_doc:
OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) control coverage. Each control maps to code-detectable signals (manifest_deps, imports, paths, terms) and carries a remediation field shown when a control is Partial or Not Compliant.

Signal design principles (informed by benchmark against OWASP agent_security_initiative corpus):
  - manifest_deps / imports must name dedicated mitigation libraries, not general ML frameworks
  - terms must be specific to the mitigation pattern, not generic words that also appear in attack
    descriptions (sandbox, DVC, HITL, prompt_injection, PII, PHI, etc. are excluded for this reason)
  - LLM08: using a vector store ≠ securing it; only access-control-specific terms are kept
  - LLM09: RAG framework presence IS a genuine misinformation mitigation signal

controls.title: Prompt Injection — input filtering and privilege separation
remediation: Add an input/output filtering layer (llm-guard, guardrails-ai, or NeMo Guardrails) between user input and the model.
Constrain model behavior in the system prompt: define role, capabilities, and limitations explicitly.
Grant the LLM only minimum necessary permissions; handle privileged operations in deterministic code, not inside the model context.
Require human-in-the-loop approval for any high-risk or irreversible actions the model can trigger.
Segregate untrusted external content (RAG docs, tool output, user messages) from trusted system instructions.
Run regular adversarial prompt-injection red-team exercises and add regression tests for discovered vectors.

controls.title: Sensitive Information Disclosure — PII redaction and access controls
remediation: Integrate a PII/PHI detection and redaction library (e.g. Presidio, scrubadub) before training data and before logging model inputs/outputs.
Apply strict access controls on training data: only grant access to data that is necessary for the specific user or process.
Limit model access to external data sources; ensure runtime data orchestration is securely managed.
Add noise via differential privacy techniques when training on sensitive datasets.
Implement pattern-matching redaction to strip confidential content (API keys, credentials, personal data) before it reaches the model.
Conceal system preamble; do not embed credentials or internal configuration details in system prompts.

controls.title: Supply Chain — SBOM, model provenance, and dependency integrity
remediation: Generate and maintain a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for all dependencies; evaluate CycloneDX ML-BOM format for AI/ML components.
Source models exclusively from verifiable repositories; use file-hash or cryptographic signing to verify model integrity before loading.
Set trust_remote_code=False (or equivalent) when loading models; audit any code executed on download.
Vet data vendors rigorously; review supplier security posture and terms regularly.
Maintain a dependency patching policy and automate CVE scanning in CI (audit-ci, Snyk, Dependabot).
Run anomaly detection and adversarial robustness testing on supplied models and datasets as part of your MLOps pipeline.

controls.title: Data and Model Poisoning — provenance tracking and anomaly detection
remediation: Track data origins and transformations with a provenance tool (MLflow, DVC, CycloneDX ML-BOM) and verify legitimacy at every pipeline stage.
Implement strict sandboxing to limit model exposure to unverified data sources during training and inference.
Use anomaly detection (Alibi Detect, Evidently, WhyLogs) to filter out adversarial or out-of-distribution training examples.
Conduct red-team adversarial robustness testing before deploying fine-tuned models; treat supplied datasets as untrusted.
Monitor training loss and behavioral metrics; set thresholds that alert on anomalous output patterns indicative of poisoning.
Store user-supplied information separately from core training data; use RAG grounding to reduce reliance on potentially poisoned weights.

controls.title: Improper Output Handling — encoding, sanitization, and zero-trust output
remediation: Treat all LLM output as untrusted input — validate and sanitize it before passing it to any backend function or rendering it in the UI.
Apply context-specific output encoding: HTML-escape for web rendering, parameterized queries for any LLM output used in SQL.
Use DOMPurify (JS) or bleach (Python) to strip active content from LLM-generated HTML before rendering.
Implement a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) to prevent execution of injected scripts in LLM-generated content.
Never use string concatenation to build SQL, shell commands, or template strings from LLM output; always use parameterized forms.
Add structured logging and monitoring for LLM output to detect unusual patterns that may indicate compromise.

controls.title: Excessive Agency — least-privilege tools and human-in-the-loop controls
remediation: Limit LLM agents to the minimum set of tools and functions necessary for the task; remove or disable all others.
Avoid open-ended extensions (shell execution, arbitrary URL fetch); replace with narrow, scoped function calls.
Track user authorization scope and execute downstream actions in the user's own security context, not the agent's elevated context.
Require human approval before any high-impact or irreversible action (delete, send, publish, transfer).
Implement authorization in downstream systems, not inside the LLM prompt; the model must never be the sole access-control decision point.
Log all agent tool invocations and apply rate limiting to constrain the frequency of undesirable actions.

controls.title: System Prompt Leakage — guardrails and external secret management
remediation: Never embed API keys, auth credentials, database names, or user role/permission structures directly in system prompts; move them to an external secrets manager.
Do not rely solely on system-prompt instructions for critical security behavior (access control, content policy); enforce these deterministically outside the model.
Implement an independent guardrail layer (guardrails-ai, NeMo Guardrails, llm-guard) that inspects output regardless of model instructions.
Treat the system prompt as potentially extractable; design the application so that exposure of the prompt does not itself constitute a security breach.
Conduct adversarial testing specifically for system prompt extraction attempts as part of your regular red-team exercises.

controls.title: Vector and Embedding Weaknesses — access control, validation, and audit logs
remediation: Implement fine-grained access controls on vector/embedding stores; enforce strict logical partitioning so users cannot retrieve embeddings from other tenants or higher-privilege namespaces.
Validate and authenticate all data sources before ingestion into the knowledge base; reject or quarantine content from unverified sources.
Regularly audit the knowledge base for hidden codes, adversarially crafted embeddings, or data poisoning artifacts.
Tag and classify data within the vector store to control access levels and prevent data mismatch during retrieval.
Maintain detailed, immutable retrieval activity logs to detect and respond promptly to anomalous query patterns.

controls.title: Misinformation — RAG grounding, fact-checking, and content labelling
remediation: Ground LLM responses in a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline backed by verified, authoritative sources to reduce hallucination.
Implement automatic output validation for high-stakes responses (e.g., medical, legal, financial) using a secondary verification step.
Clearly label all AI-generated content in the UI and communicate known limitations and the possibility of inaccuracies to users.
Implement human oversight and fact-checking processes for critical or sensitive outputs before they reach end users.
Use chain-of-thought prompting and require the model to cite sources; evaluate faithfulness with RAGAs or TruLens.
Establish secure coding practices to prevent integration of vulnerabilities due to incorrect LLM code suggestions.

controls.title: Unbounded Consumption — rate limiting, input size limits, and resource controls
remediation: Implement strict input validation with a maximum input size limit to prevent resource exhaustion from oversized payloads.
Apply per-user and per-IP rate limiting and quotas; restrict the number of requests a single source entity can make in a given time period.
Set request timeouts and throttle processing for resource-intensive LLM operations to prevent prolonged resource consumption.
Restrict the LLM's access to network resources, internal services, and APIs using a sandboxed execution environment.
Monitor resource usage continuously and implement anomaly detection to identify and respond to unusual consumption patterns.
Design the system to degrade gracefully under heavy load, maintaining partial functionality rather than complete failure.
Restrict or obfuscate logit_bias and logprobs in API responses to limit model extraction and distillation attacks.
